questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual memory and how it allows programs larger than physical memory to execute.
keyPoints: Virtual memory extends physical RAM using disk space
Uses paging or segmentation to divide logical and physical memory
Pages swapped between disk and RAM on demand
Enables programs to use address space larger than physical memory
Reduces actual physical memory requirements

questions.question: Differentiate between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation with examples.
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: unused memory within allocated block
Example: 8KB process gets 12KB frame, 4KB wasted inside
External fragmentation: free memory scattered in non-contiguous blocks
Example: three free blocks of 2MB each but cannot allocate 5MB contiguously
Paging causes internal, contiguous allocation causes external

questions.question: Describe the FIFO page replacement algorithm with an example scenario showing page faults.
keyPoints: FIFO evicts the oldest page in memory
Uses queue data structure to track page arrival order
Simple to implement but may replace heavily used pages
Example: frames=3, sequence: 1,2,3,4,1,2,5 shows how faults occur
Can suffer from Belady's anomaly

questions.question: What is thrashing? Explain its causes and effects on system performance.
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive paging activity degrading performance
Cause: processes do not have enough frames for working set
Effect: CPU spends time swapping pages instead of executing
High page fault rate and low CPU utilization are symptoms
Solution: working set model, page fault frequency scheme

questions.question: Explain the purpose and functioning of the Memory Management Unit (MMU).
keyPoints: MMU is hardware for address translation
Converts virtual addresses to physical addresses
Uses page tables for translation
Provides memory protection between processes
May include TLB for faster translation

questions.question: Compare paging and segmentation based on: (i) fragmentation type (ii) address structure (iii) user view.
keyPoints: Paging: fixed-size, internal fragmentation, no logical user view
Segmentation: variable-size, external fragmentation, logical user view
Paging: address = page number + offset
Segmentation: address = segment number + offset
Segmentation provides protection per segment, paging uses fixed protections

questions.question: Explain demand paging and describe the steps when a page fault occurs.
keyPoints: Demand paging loads pages only when referenced
Steps: 1) Check if page in memory 2) If not, trap to OS 3) Find free frame 4) Read page from disk to frame 5) Update page table 6) Restart instruction
Reduces initial load time and memory usage
First reference to any page causes page fault

questions.question: Describe the LRU (Least Recently Used) page replacement algorithm. Explain why it is difficult to implement exactly and discuss any approximation techniques.
keyPoints: LRU evicts page not used for longest time
Assumes recently used pages will be used again soon
Implementation needs hardware support to track usage order
Stack algorithm - pages in memory form a stack of recency
Counter implementation: each page has counter updated on access
Stack algorithm: pages in set form decreasing timestamp order
Approximations: Second Chance, Clock algorithm, NFU
Does not suffer from Belady's anomaly
Better than FIFO but more overhead

questions.question: Explain the working set model of memory management. How does it help in controlling thrashing?
keyPoints: Working set: set of pages process is actively using
Defined by window (W): pages referenced in last W references
Process needs minimum working set frames to function efficiently
If allocated frames < working set size → thrashing
Working set model: maintain adequate frames per process
Thrashing prevention: monitor page fault frequency
If fault rate too high → increase allocated frames
If fault rate too low → decrease allocated frames
Balance between multiprogramming degree and thrashing

questions.question: Explain the concept of paging in detail. Include discussion on page tables, TLB, inverted page tables, and multi-level paging.
keyPoints: Paging divides memory into fixed-size frames and pages
Logical address: page number + offset
Physical address: frame number + offset
Page table maps pages to frames, stored in main memory
TLB caches recent translations for fast access
Inverted page table: one entry per physical frame
Multi-level paging: hierarchical page tables for large address spaces
Page table entry contains: frame number, valid bit, protection bits
Dirty bit for modified pages before eviction

questions.question: Compare FIFO, LRU, and Optimal page replacement algorithms with respect to: (i) implementation complexity (ii) performance (iii) Belady's anomaly.
keyPoints: FIFO: simple queue implementation, moderate performance, suffers Belady's anomaly
LRU: tracks usage history, better performance, no Belady's anomaly, harder to implement
Optimal: replaces page not used longest in future, best performance, no Belady's anomaly, impossible to implement practically
FIFO can be outperformed by increasing frames (anomaly)
LRU uses stack property - more frames always means fewer faults
Optimal used as theoretical benchmark only
